title: Verbier or Zermatt?
dek: Two of Switzerland’s greatest resorts, two very different weeks. Which one is yours.
sub: Two of Switzerland’s greatest resorts, two very different weeks. Which one is yours.
lead: Verbier and Zermatt are both among the best resorts in the world, both unmistakably Swiss, and both likely to leave you happy. But they ask for different things and offer different weeks, and the choice between them comes down to temperament more than terrain. Here is how to decide.

sections.title: The mountains
paras: Verbier is a freerider’s resort. The terrain is steep, the off-piste is legendary, and the mountain rewards confidence — the Bec des Rosses does not host the Xtreme final by accident. Intermediates ski well here, but Verbier’s soul is in its off-piste, and it skis best for those who can use it.

Zermatt is higher, vaster and more varied, with the highest skiing in the Alps and snow-sure terrain spread across three sectors and over the border into Italy. It suits cruising intermediates and experts alike, and the sheer scale means a week barely scratches it. The Matterhorn watches over all of it.

sections.title: The towns
paras: Verbier is a buzzy, monied, international resort — a real après scene, serious restaurants, and an energy that builds through the afternoon and runs into the night. It is a place that likes a good time and has the crowd to provide one.

Zermatt is a car-free mountain town with more romance and less noise — a working village with centuries of mountaineering history, horse-drawn sleighs in the snow, and a quieter, more grown-up tempo. It is the more beautiful of the two by some distance, and the more serene.

figure.alt: The Matterhorn above Zermatt, Switzerland — Vertige
figure.cap: The Matterhorn above Zermatt, Switzerland — Vertige

sections.title: Getting there
paras: Verbier is the easier transfer — around an hour and a half to two hours from Geneva, mostly motorway with a climb at the end. Zermatt takes longer and ends in a train, since the town is car-free: you drive to Täsch and ride the last stretch up. The journey to Zermatt is part of its character, but it is a journey.

sections.title: Who each suits
paras: Choose Verbier if you ski hard, want off-piste at the centre of the week, and like a resort with energy and a late evening. Choose Zermatt if you want scale, snow certainty, a more beautiful and peaceful town, and terrain that suits a mixed group of abilities. One is a night out; the other is a long, romantic dinner.

sections.title: The verdict
paras: For a group of strong skiers who want adrenaline and atmosphere, Verbier. For almost everyone else — families, mixed abilities, anyone who values the setting and the serenity as much as the skiing — Zermatt. If you can only do one and you are undecided, Zermatt is the safer bet: bigger, higher, more beautiful, and kinder to a varied group.

pick.text: Undecided? Choose Zermatt. It is higher and more snow-sure, the terrain suits a wider range of abilities, and the car-free town beneath the Matterhorn is simply one of the loveliest places to spend a week in the Alps. Save Verbier for the year your group is all strong skiers chasing off-piste and a proper night out — it is unbeatable for that, and overqualified for anything gentler.

title: Méribel, Beyond the Obvious
dek: The runs, tables and corners of Méribel that the week-one visitor walks straight past.
sub: The runs, tables and corners of Méribel that the week-one visitor walks straight past.
lead: Méribel is the heart of the Trois Vallées, which means most visitors spend the week using it as a junction — racing off to Courchevel or Val Thorens and back. Stay a beat longer and the valley reveals what the through-traffic misses: quieter trees, better tables, and a couple of corners worth planning a day around.

sections.title: The runs people skip
paras: Everyone funnels onto the main motorways between the valleys. The reward for getting off them is real. The tree-lined runs down towards the lower villages hold their best skiing in flat light and bad weather, when the high open bowls are a whiteout — local knowledge that turns a written-off day into a good one.

The north-facing slopes above the valley keep their snow long after the sunny sides have gone heavy. Late in the day and late in the season, that is where the skiing stays good while everyone else complains about the slush.

sections.title: Where the locals eat
paras: The famous mountain restaurants take the bookings and the photographs. The better value — and often the better lunch — is at the smaller huts off the main pistes, the ones without a view to sell and a kitchen that has to try harder. We keep a list, and it is not the one in the guidebooks.

figure.alt: A quiet tree-lined run above Méribel — Vertige
figure.cap: A quiet tree-lined run above Méribel — Vertige

sections.title: The Trois Vallées trick
paras: Méribel’s real asset is its position. From the middle of the largest linked ski area in the world, you can ski to breakfast in one valley and lunch in another. The trick the locals know is timing — heading to Courchevel or Val Thorens against the flow, early or late, so you get the terrain without the queues that catch everyone leaving at nine.

sections.title: Off the mountain
paras: Méribel’s evenings are quieter and more grown-up than its Trois Vallées neighbours, which is precisely the point for the people who come back. It is a family resort that has aged well — less about the bass line, more about a good dinner and an early start. The hidden gem is the resort’s whole temperament.

pick.text: Give Méribel a day where you do not leave the valley chasing the rest of the Trois Vallées — ski the north-facing trees in the morning, take lunch at one of the smaller huts off the main piste, and you will see why people who could ski anywhere keep coming back here. Then, when you do head for Courchevel or Val Thorens, go early and against the crowd. Méribel rewards the skier who slows down in the middle of the busiest ski area on earth.

title: Alpine Wellness, Out of Season
dek: The quiet weeks are when the spa, the slow morning and the long sauna actually come into their own.
sub: The quiet weeks are when the spa, the slow morning and the long sauna actually come into their own.
lead: There is a version of a ski holiday that is not really about the skiing. It is about the cold air and the hot water, the sauna after a few runs, the massage that undoes the first day’s stiffness, and the long unhurried morning. In the quiet weeks of the season, that version comes into its own.

sections.title: Why quiet season suits it
paras: Wellness needs space and calm, and peak weeks offer neither. In January after the holidays, or the quieter stretches of spring, the resort exhales — the spas are not booked out, the pools are not full, and the rhythm of the day slows to something restorative. You can ski for the morning and do nothing of consequence for the afternoon.

sections.title: The chalet as the spa
paras: At this level, the best wellness is often in the chalet itself — a private sauna, a hot tub on the terrace, a pool you do not share, a masseuse who comes to you. No booking a slot, no robe in a corridor. The mountain provides the cold; the chalet provides the warm. For a certain kind of week, that is the whole appeal.

figure.alt: A private chalet sauna with mountain views, the Alps — Vertige
figure.cap: A private chalet sauna with mountain views, the Alps — Vertige

sections.title: Skiing optional
paras: The quiet weeks suit groups where not everyone is chasing vertical. One half of the party skis hard; the other half walks, swims, reads, and meets them for a long lunch. A resort with good off-mountain life — a real town, thermal baths, a few proper restaurants — makes the non-skiing days as good as the skiing ones.

[Bad Gastein](https://vertigeski.com/destinations/bad-gastein), with its thermal-spa tradition, and the Swiss resorts with serious wellness and quiet valleys, do this better than the high party resorts. The mountain does not have to be the only thing you came for.

sections.title: The case for slowing down
paras: A ski week does not have to be measured in runs. Some of the best ones are measured in mornings that started slowly, afternoons in the sauna, and dinners that ran long. The quiet season is permission to take that week — the mountain is still there, just calmer, and so is everyone on it.

pick.text: If wellness is the point, go in a quiet week and let the chalet do the work — a private sauna, a terrace hot tub and a masseuse who comes to you beat any resort spa you have to book and share. Pair it with a resort that has genuine off-mountain life, like Bad Gastein, so the non-skiing days have somewhere to go. Ski the mornings, sauna the afternoons, and do not count the runs.

title: The Best Resorts for Beginners
dek: Where to learn to ski without being put off it — chosen for gentle terrain, good schools, and somewhere to graduate to.
sub: Where to learn to ski without being put off it — chosen for gentle terrain, good schools, and somewhere to graduate to.
lead: The first week decides whether someone skis for life or never again. Get the resort wrong — too steep, too high, too intimidating — and a beginner spends five days cold, frightened and quietly resolving never to return.

sections.title: What actually makes a resort good for beginners
paras: Three things matter. Gentle, accessible nursery slopes near the village, so the first days are not a logistical ordeal. A strong English-speaking ski school with enough instructors that group sizes stay small. And — easily forgotten — somewhere to progress to, so the second week is not a wall.

Altitude and snow reliability matter too, but less than people think for a beginner. A nervous learner on a gentle blue in good light does not need a glacier. They need terrain that builds confidence, not adrenaline.

sections.title: Where we send first-timers
paras: [La Tania](https://vertigeski.com/destinations/la-tania) and the lower Courchevel villages. Wooded, gentle, and part of the vast Trois Vallées — so a beginner learns somewhere forgiving and graduates onto endless terrain without ever changing resort.

[Lech](https://vertigeski.com/destinations/lech). Gentle, sunny nursery areas, an exceptional ski-school tradition, and a village that rewards the non-skiing afternoons. It skis far easier than its glamorous reputation suggests.

[Megève](https://vertigeski.com/destinations/megeve). Mellow, tree-lined and characterful, with a real town to enjoy off the snow. One of the kindest places in the Alps to learn, and lovely for a family where not everyone is sold on skiing yet.

figure.alt: A first ski lesson on a gentle slope, the Alps — Vertige
figure.cap: A first ski lesson on a gentle slope, the Alps — Vertige

sections.title: Where not to learn
paras: Some great resorts are poor places to start. Chamonix is a serious mountain with limited gentle terrain in the centre — extraordinary, but not for week one. Val Thorens is reliable but high and exposed, which can be bleak for a nervous beginner. The point is not that these are lesser resorts. They are simply the wrong first classroom.

sections.title: The family angle
paras: Most beginners in this market are learning alongside children who are also learning. That changes the calculation: you want a resort where ski school, nursery slopes and the chalet sit close together, so the day does not turn into a transport exercise. Proximity beats prestige every time in the first week.

pick.text: For a true first-timer — adult or child — start in the lower Trois Vallées around La Tania. It is gentle enough to build confidence in the first few days, English-speaking instruction is plentiful, and the moment a beginner is ready for more, the largest linked ski area in the world is right there. You learn somewhere forgiving and never have to leave to progress. Book a chalet within walking distance of the ski school and the first week looks after itself.

title: Why a Chalet, Not a Hotel
dek: The case for taking the whole building — and what you actually gain when you do.
h1: Why a Chalet, Not a Hotel
sub: The case for taking the whole building — and what you actually gain when you do.
lead: A hotel sells you a room. A chalet gives you a building. For one week a year, with the people you most want to spend it with, that difference turns out to matter more than almost anything else on the trip.

sections.title: The space is the point
paras: A chalet is yours for the week. The kitchen, the living room, the boot room, the hot tub on the terrace — no sharing, no booking a slot, no other guests. For a family or a group, that privacy is the experience: children can be loud, teenagers can sprawl, and the adults can talk without lowering their voices.

A hotel suite, however large, is still a room in someone else’s building. You step into shared corridors and shared restaurants the moment you leave it. For some trips — a couple, a short break, a city-and-ski combination — that is exactly right. For a group taking a week together, it rarely is.

sections.title: Catering on your terms
paras: The biggest practical difference is food. A catered chalet comes with a chef who cooks around your day, not the kitchen’s hours. Breakfast when the first skiers are up, children fed early, a proper dinner once they’re down. No reservations, no dressing for the dining room, no rationing the good wine.

Hotels do breakfast and dinner well, but on their schedule. If your group’s rhythm is its own — early starts, late dinners, a long lunch that runs into the afternoon — a chalet bends to it in a way a hotel cannot.

figure.alt: A chalet dinner with the whole group, the Alps — Vertige
figure.cap: A chalet dinner with the whole group, the Alps — Vertige

sections.title: Where a hotel still wins
paras: Honesty matters here. A hotel makes sense for smaller groups, shorter stays, and anyone who wants a spa, a concierge desk and room service without committing to a whole property. A couple after a quiet long weekend is often better served by a great hotel than a six-bedroom chalet they will rattle around in.
It also suits the traveller who wants no domestic footprint at all — just a key and a turned-down bed. That is a real preference, and a hotel answers it perfectly.

sections.title: The cost question
paras: At the top of the market the headline numbers converge, but the maths differs. A chalet’s cost is shared across the group, and a single large property for three families often works out below three hotel suites — with a private chef included. A hotel’s cost is per room, with extras billed as you go.
The right comparison is not nightly rate against nightly rate. It is the whole week, for your whole group, with everything you will actually use.

pick.text: For a family or a group of friends taking a full week, the chalet wins almost every time — the privacy and the catering are the holiday, not the backdrop. Keep the hotel in mind for two people on a short break, or for a night either side of the transfer. If you are deciding for a group of six or more, take the building. You will use every room of it, and the week will be yours in a way a hotel cannot match.

title: Easter in the Alps
dek: Spring sun, long days, and a holiday week that still skis well — at the resorts that hold their snow.
sub: Spring sun, long days, and a holiday week that still skis well — at the resorts that hold their snow.
lead: Easter is the season’s last hurrah, and at the right resort it is one of its best weeks. The light is long, the sun has warmth, the terraces are full in the good way — and the skiing, up high, is still excellent. The trick is being up high.

sections.title: The appeal
paras: Spring skiing is forgiving and sociable. Firm pistes in the morning soften pleasantly by lunch, the days stretch past four o’clock, and the whole week runs at a gentler tempo than mid-winter. It suits families and mixed-ability groups especially well — the conditions are kind.

Because Easter moves between late March and late April, the experience shifts year to year. An early Easter skis like deep winter; a late one is full spring. Worth knowing which you are getting before you choose the resort.

sections.title: Go high, or take a chance
paras: The one rule of Easter is altitude. High, snow-sure resorts ski reliably through April; lower, tree-lined ones become a gamble as the season tires. This late, height is not a luxury — it is the difference between a great week and a slushy one.

figure.alt: Easter sunshine on a high mountain, the Alps — Vertige
figure.cap: Easter sunshine on a high mountain, the Alps — Vertige

sections.title: Where Easter works
paras: [Val Thorens](https://vertigeski.com/destinations/val-thorens), [Tignes](https://vertigeski.com/destinations/tignes) and Obergurgl. The high-altitude insurance policy — snow-sure into late April, with glacier terrain to fall back on.

[Zermatt](https://vertigeski.com/destinations/zermatt) and [Val d’Isère](https://vertigeski.com/destinations/val-disere). Serious altitude with great towns, so the long spring evenings have somewhere to go.
The high Arlberg — [Lech](https://vertigeski.com/destinations/lech) and Zürs. Reliable late snow and a village that is at its most relaxed once peak season has passed.

sections.title: A family week
paras: Easter is a family holiday, so the better resorts fill — book as you would for half-term. But the mood is softer than February: warmer, calmer, more forgiving for younger skiers and grandparents alike. For many families it is quietly the best week of the year.

pick.text: For Easter, buy the altitude insurance: Val Thorens or Tignes if snow certainty is everything, or Zermatt for the same reliability with a great town for the long evenings. Book as early as you would for any holiday week, and choose a chalet with a terrace that catches the afternoon sun — at Easter, that terrace is half the holiday.

title: The Family Ski Holiday, Age by Age
dek: The toddler year and the teenage year are different trips. How to plan for the children you actually have.
h1: The Family Ski Holiday, Age by Age
sub: The toddler year and the teenage year are different trips. How to plan for the children you actually have.
lead: There is no such thing as “a family ski holiday” in the abstract. There is the year your eldest is three and the whole trip bends around nap times, and there is the year they are fifteen and want nothing to do with you until dinner. Plan for the children you have, not a generic family.

sections.title: The youngest years (under six)
paras: The trip runs on the smallest person’s timetable. Ski school usually takes children from around three, in short sessions, which means your skiing happens in windows rather than full days. The most underrated requirement is childcare — a nanny, or a chalet with one — to cover the gap and give the parents an afternoon run.

Choose a resort where everything is close: nursery slopes, ski-school meeting point and chalet within walking distance. With a toddler in ski boots, a 400-metre walk is a negotiation. Gentle, low-stress resorts win; the big serious mountains can wait.

sections.title: The middle years (seven to twelve)
paras: This is the sweet spot. Children ski properly now, improve fast, and can take full days in ski school or with the family. The resort can get more ambitious — more terrain, longer runs, the first taste of skiing together as a group.

It is also the age where a great instructor changes everything. The right one turns a competent seven-year-old into a confident ten-year-old who will happily follow you down a red. We spend real effort finding the instructors who are good with this age, not just available.

figure.alt: A family skiing together, the Alps — Vertige
figure.cap: A family skiing together, the Alps — Vertige

sections.title: The teenage years (thirteen and up)
paras: Teenagers want independence and a peer group, not a family ski lesson. The trip succeeds when they have terrain to roam, the freedom to ski with friends or siblings, and a corner of the chalet that is theirs after dinner. A resort with a bit of life off the mountain helps.

The mistake is planning a teenage trip like a young-children trip. Give them room and they will surprise you — and still turn up for the long lunch and the family dinner, which is all you really wanted.

sections.title: The chalet matters more than the resort
paras: Across every age, the building does the heavy lifting. Space for different rhythms, a room that works for the youngest and one for the oldest, a chef who can feed children at five and adults at eight. Get the chalet right and a family holiday runs itself; get it wrong and someone spends the week managing.
It is the same case for multi-generational weeks, where grandparents join the mix — a subject worth its own piece.

pick.text: Whatever the age, choose proximity over prestige. A resort where ski school, nursery slopes and the chalet are within walking distance removes the single biggest source of family-holiday friction — the daily logistics of getting everyone to the right place. For mixed ages, the lower Trois Vallées and Lech both do this unusually well. Spend the saved energy on the skiing, not the shuttling.

title: February Half-Term, Sorted
dek: The busiest, brightest week of the season — how to do it well, and what to book early.
sub: The busiest, brightest week of the season — how to do it well, and what to book early.
lead: February half-term is the high tide of the ski season. The mountains are full, the snow is usually at its best, and every good chalet in every good resort is spoken for months ahead. It is the week most families can travel — which is exactly why it takes the most planning.

sections.title: Why it is worth it anyway
paras: Mid-winter conditions are reliable, the days are lengthening, and there is an energy to a resort in full swing that the quiet weeks do not have. For families tied to the school calendar, it is often the one real ski week of the year — and done well, it is superb.

sections.title: Book early, genuinely early
paras: This is not a week to leave late. The best chalets for half-term are reserved a year ahead, sometimes by the same families returning annually. If you want a specific property — or any property of a certain size — the time to act is the spring or summer before, not the autumn.
Flights and transfers compress too. Saturday changeover days are the busiest of the season on the transfer roads; a private transfer and a well-timed flight are worth more this week than any other.

figure.alt: A bright February half-term day on the mountain, the Alps — Vertige
figure.cap: A bright February half-term day on the mountain, the Alps — Vertige

sections.title: Choosing a resort for the crowds
paras: Big linked areas absorb numbers better than small ones. The Trois Vallées, the Espace Killy and the Arlberg have the lift capacity and the terrain to spread the week’s crowds; smaller resorts can feel pinched. If you want space at peak week, go somewhere large.
Ski-in, ski-out matters more this week than any other — avoiding the morning lift queue with children is worth a great deal when the resort is full.

sections.title: The ski-school problem
paras: Ski schools fill as fast as the chalets. Children’s group lessons for half-term sell out early, and the good private instructors are booked solid. This is the single thing most families leave too late. Securing instruction should happen the moment the chalet is confirmed.

pick.text: If half-term is your week, commit early and go big — a large, well-connected area like the [Trois Vallées](https://vertigeski.com/destinations/meribel) will swallow the crowds better than a boutique resort, and a ski-in, ski-out chalet removes the worst of the peak-week friction. Lock in children’s ski school the day you book the chalet, not the month before you travel. The families who do half-term brilliantly are simply the ones who booked it first.

title: Planning Your First Luxury Ski Holiday
dek: A short, opinionated guide to getting the big decisions right — resort, chalet, timing and service.
h1: Planning Your First Luxury Ski Holiday
sub: A short, opinionated guide to getting the big decisions right — resort, chalet, timing and service.
lead: A luxury ski holiday is one of the few trips where the planning genuinely determines the week. Get the four big decisions right — where, what, when and who arranges it — and everything else follows. Get them wrong and no amount of thread count will fix it.

sections.title: First, the resort
paras: Start with the group, not the brochure. A resort that is perfect for an expert couple can be wrong for a family of beginners, and the reverse. Match the terrain to how your group actually skis, and weigh what you want off the mountain — a real town, or somewhere purpose-built and ski-in, ski-out.

If you are choosing between two famous names, that is usually a comparison worth making properly. We write those head-to-head — Verbier or Zermatt, Val d’Isère or Verbier — because the right answer depends entirely on you.

sections.title: Then, the chalet
paras: The property is the holiday more than the resort is. Prioritise layout over photographs: how the rooms work for your group, where the bedrooms sit, how close the lift is. A beautiful chalet with the wrong layout for your group is the wrong chalet.
Decide early on the things that are hard to add later — ski-in, ski-out access, a pool, a chef, enough separation for different ages. These shape the shortlist more than the interior styling.

figure.alt: A chalet interior with a mountain view, the Alps — Vertige
figure.cap: A chalet interior with a mountain view, the Alps — Vertige

sections.title: Then, the timing
paras: When you go shapes the whole trip. Peak weeks — Christmas, February half-term — bring atmosphere and crowds and the highest prices. The quieter weeks of January and late March offer the same mountain at a different pace. Be honest about whether you are tied to school holidays, and if you are not, use the freedom.

sections.title: Finally, who arranges it
paras: The right operator does the part you cannot: knows the resorts well enough to steer you, reads the chalets honestly, and handles the logistics — transfers, instruction, reservations — so the week runs without you managing it. The wrong one hands you a list and a price.

That is the whole difference, and it is worth choosing for. A good operator should have an opinion, tell you when you have picked the wrong chalet, and make the week feel like it was built around you — because it was.

pick.text: Decide the group’s priorities before you look at a single property. Write down how everyone skis, what you want off the mountain, and the one or two things the chalet absolutely must have. Hand that to an operator who knows the resorts and let them steer — that conversation, had properly once, saves you a hundred browser tabs and gets you a week that actually fits. Start there, not with the photographs.

title: The Case for March and April
dek: Longer days, quieter pistes, and the most civilised skiing of the season — if you know what you are choosing.
h1: The Case for March and April
sub: Longer days, quieter pistes, and the most civilised skiing of the season — if you know what you are choosing.
lead: By March the mountains exhale. The school holidays have passed, the light has lengthened, and the frantic energy of peak season gives way to something more civilised: long days, warm terraces, and pistes you do not have to share.

sections.title: What you gain
paras: The days are long. By late March there is skiable light from early morning until well past four, which changes the whole rhythm — unhurried breakfasts, a proper lunch, and afternoons that do not feel rushed. The sun has warmth, and the terraces come into their own.

The mountain is quieter. Lift queues thin, the good tables open up, and instructors and guides have time. For a group that values the experience over the box-ticking, it is the most relaxed week of the winter.

sections.title: The honest trade-off
paras: Snow is the variable. Lower resorts can soften by afternoon and tire by late April, and conditions become a question of altitude and aspect rather than a given. This is the one time of year where choosing the right resort genuinely matters more than when you book.

The answer is height. Snow-sure, high-altitude resorts ski beautifully deep into spring — firm in the morning, forgiving by lunch — while lower, tree-lined resorts are a gamble you do not need to take this late.

figure.alt: A spring afternoon on a high mountain terrace, the Alps — Vertige
figure.cap: A spring afternoon on a high mountain terrace, the Alps — Vertige

sections.title: Where to go late
paras: [Val Thorens](https://vertigeski.com/destinations/val-thorens) and [Tignes](https://vertigeski.com/destinations/tignes). The highest resorts in the Alps, snow-sure into spring, with glacier terrain that holds when everywhere lower has softened. The safe late-season bets.

[Zermatt](https://vertigeski.com/destinations/zermatt) and [Val d’Isère](https://vertigeski.com/destinations/val-disere). High, serious and reliable, with the altitude to ski well in April and the towns to enjoy the long evenings.
The high Arlberg — [Lech](https://vertigeski.com/destinations/lech) and Zürs. Austrian altitude with the village character that peak-season crowds otherwise obscure.

sections.title: Easter, specifically
paras: Easter is the one late-season week that bucks the quiet — it is a family holiday, and the better resorts fill. It deserves its own planning, and we have written about it separately. The weeks either side of it are where the real low-season calm lives.

pick.text: If you want the best of late season, go high and go in the weeks around — not on — Easter. Val Thorens or Tignes for snow certainty, or Zermatt if you want the altitude with a great town attached. Book a chalet with a south-facing terrace; in spring, the part of the day you remember might be the long lunch in the sun as much as the skiing itself.

title: The multi-generational ski week, done properly
dek: Three generations, three completely different holidays, one chalet that has to hold all of them. Here is what actually makes it work.
h1: The multi-generational ski week, done properly
sub: Three generations, three completely different holidays, one chalet that has to hold all of them. Here is what actually makes it work.

sections.title: The chalet does the heavy lifting
paras: The single most important decision is not the resort or the snow. It is the building. A hotel asks everyone to live to the same timetable — the same breakfast hours, the same dining room, the same lift. A good chalet lets the holidays run in parallel and meet when people want them to.

What that requires is specific. Enough separation that a 6am toddler and a 1am teenager are not sharing a wall. A second sitting room or a media room, so that “together” stays a choice rather than an obligation. And at least one bedroom that works for grandparents who would rather not climb three flights in ski socks — ground floor, or near the lift if there is one. When we shortlist for a multi-generational group, we study the floorplan before the photographs.

sections.title: Three generations, three different holidays
paras: The toddler year and the teenage year are not the same trip, and a chalet that suits one can quietly defeat the other.

The youngest set the morning timetable. Ski school usually runs until mid-afternoon, which leaves a gap before the adults are off the mountain — the single most underestimated logistics problem of the week. Teenagers want independence, a later evening and a corner of the chalet that is theirs. Grandparents may want two good runs, a sunny terrace and a wine list — or no skiing at all, in which case a real town to walk into matters far more than the size of the ski area.
Few resorts serve all three equally well. The ones that do are worth choosing deliberately, which is most of what this decision comes down to.

sections.title: The things that matter more than they sound
paras: Ski-in, ski-out — or close to it. With small children and older legs in the same group, the walk to the lift is not a detail. Carrying skis and a four-year-old 400 metres in ski boots is the difference between a calm morning and a daily argument. For a multi-generational group it matters more than for any other.

A private chef. Feeding twelve people across three generations and two bedtimes is the biggest source of friction in a big group. A chef who can put children’s supper out at five and serve the adults properly at eight removes it entirely, and gives the parents back the part of the day they would otherwise lose.
A driver. The transfer from the airport with a toddler, a teenager and a grandparent is where a week starts well or badly. It is worth getting right before anything else.

figure.alt: Ski-in, ski-out access from the chalet door, the Alps — Vertige
figure.cap: Ski-in, ski-out access from the chalet door, the Alps — Vertige

sections.title: Where the concierge earns its place
paras: The honest version is that most of what makes a multi-generational week work is invisible and faintly unglamorous. Booking four children into three different ability groups in the same time slot. Finding the one instructor who is genuinely good with a nervous eight-year-old. Covering the afternoon gap with a nanny. Holding a table for eleven that does not mind a high chair. Sorting lift passes before anyone arrives, so nobody queues with a pram.
None of it photographs well. All of it is the difference between a parent who relaxes and a parent who manages. That is the part we take off your hands.
Study the floorplan before the photographs. With three generations, the layout decides the holiday.

sections.title: Where to go
paras: Some resorts are built for this and some quietly resist it. Lech is close to ideal — gentle and serious terrain side by side, a great deal of it ski-in, ski-out, and a real village rather than a purpose-built station. Méribel sits in the middle of the Trois Vallées, with tree-lined runs for nervous skiers and effectively unlimited terrain for the teenagers who want to roam.

Val d’Isère pairs an excellent ski school with a proper town a non-skiing grandparent can enjoy on foot. And for the youngest, the wooded, gentle runs around La Tania and Courchevel’s lower villages take the fear out of the first week. The right answer depends on your particular group — which is exactly the conversation worth having before you book.
• /destinations/lech   • /destinations/meribel   • /destinations/val-disere   • /destinations/la-tania   • /service (Reserve & Privé)   • /approach   • /concierge

pick.text: If it is your first multi-generational winter and you want to load the odds in your favour, go to Lech. It is the rare resort where a nervous beginner, an off-piste teenager and a grandparent who wants one good run and a long lunch can each have their ideal day and still meet for dinner — and because so much of it is ski-in, ski-out, the logistics that sink other multi-generational weeks barely arise. Choose the chalet for its layout first and its view second. The view is a bonus; the layout is the holiday.

title: Pre-Ski Fitness: Eight Weeks Out
dek: A little preparation turns the first day from a survival exercise into a good one. What actually helps.
sub: A little preparation turns the first day from a survival exercise into a good one. What actually helps.
lead: The cruellest part of a ski holiday is that the first day is often the worst. Legs that have not skied in a year give out by mid-afternoon, the next morning is agony, and a quarter of a precious week is lost to simply getting fit on the mountain. A little preparation fixes that entirely.

sections.title: Why skiing punishes the unprepared
paras: Skiing uses the legs in a way nothing else quite does — sustained load on the quads as they control the descent, plus a core working constantly to keep you balanced. You can be generally fit and still find skiing finds muscles you had forgotten. The fix is specific, not heroic.

sections.title: The legs
paras: The single most useful thing is to build leg endurance. Squats, lunges and step-ups in the weeks before — and, crucially, holding positions, since skiing is as much about controlled stillness as movement. Wall sits are unglamorous and exactly right: they train the quads to hold the load a turn demands.

figure.alt: Preparing the legs before a ski trip — Vertige
figure.cap: Preparing the legs before a ski trip — Vertige

sections.title: The core and the balance
paras: A strong core is what keeps the upper body quiet while the legs work, and what saves you when the snow does something unexpected. Planks, side planks and any single-leg balance work pay off directly. Skiing is a balancing act performed at speed; train the balance and the skiing feels easier.

sections.title: The conditioning
paras: Altitude and full days are their own demand. Any cardiovascular base — running, cycling, swimming — means you are not gasping on the first long run, and you recover better overnight. Eight weeks of even moderate conditioning changes how the week feels from day one.

sections.title: The honest minimum
paras: If you do nothing else, do wall sits and walk more in the weeks before. It is not a training programme, but it is the difference between spending day one finding your legs and spending it skiing. The mountain rewards the small effort out of all proportion.

pick.text: Start eight weeks out and keep it simple: wall sits and lunges for the legs, planks for the core, and any cardio you will actually do for the conditioning. You do not need a gym or a programme — twenty minutes, three times a week, aimed at the legs holding a position. Do that and you will ski the first day instead of surviving it, which on a one-week holiday is a quarter of the trip reclaimed.

title: Ski-In, Ski-Out: What It Actually Buys You
dek: The most overused phrase in the brochures — and what it really means for your week.
h1: Ski-In, Ski-Out: What It Actually Buys You
sub: The most overused phrase in the brochures — and what it really means for your week.
lead: “Ski-in, ski-out” is the most claimed and least defined phrase in the ski market. It can mean a chalet with skis-on access to the lift system, or a ten-minute walk that someone has generously rounded down. The difference matters, and it is worth knowing what you are actually being sold.

sections.title: What it should mean
paras: True ski-in, ski-out means you click into your skis at the door and ski to the lift, and ski back to the door at the end of the day. No walking in ski boots, no carrying skis, no shuttle. In the morning especially, it transforms the day — you are on the mountain in minutes, not after a logistical operation.

sections.title: What it is actually worth
paras: For some groups it is a nice-to-have. For others it is the single most valuable feature of the chalet. With young children, with older skiers, or with a large group trying to leave at the same time, the walk to the lift is the daily friction point — and removing it is worth more than almost any interior feature.

It also changes the shape of the day. Slope-side, people pop back for lunch, a forgotten glove, a rest. The chalet stays part of the skiing rather than a place you leave in the morning and return to at night.

figure.alt: True ski-in, ski-out access at the chalet door, the Alps — Vertige
figure.cap: True ski-in, ski-out access at the chalet door, the Alps — Vertige

sections.title: The catch worth checking
paras: Ski-out is easy; ski-in is the part to verify. Plenty of chalets let you ski down to the door but require a lift or a walk to get back up to them at day’s end — gravity is generous in one direction only. Always check the return journey, not just the morning.
Altitude and aspect matter too: the best slope-side chalets are positioned so the access run holds snow late in the season. We check both before we call anything ski-in, ski-out.

sections.title: Who should prioritise it
paras: If your group includes small children, anyone less mobile, or simply more than six people, treat true slope-side access as a priority rather than a bonus. If you are a fit couple happy to walk five minutes, spend the premium elsewhere — on the view, the space, or the chef.

pick.text: Do not take “ski-in, ski-out” on trust — ask the specific question: can we ski to the lift in the morning, and ski back to the door at night, skis on, no walking? If the honest answer is yes and your group has children or mixed mobility, it is worth paying for. If it is a couple who do not mind a short stroll, let it go and put the money into the chalet itself. We will always tell you which it really is.

title: What to Pack for a Ski Week
dek: The short list that matters — what to bring, what to leave, and what to arrange instead of carry.
h1: What to Pack for a Ski Week
sub: The short list that matters — what to bring, what to leave, and what to arrange instead of carry.
lead: Packing for a ski week is a balance between bringing too much and forgetting the one thing that ruins a day. At this level, the goal is not to carry everything — it is to bring what matters and arrange the rest. Half of what people lug to the Alps could be waiting for them there instead.

sections.title: The layers that matter
paras: Skiing is a layering problem, not a thick-coat problem. A good base layer, a mid layer you can add or shed, and a quality outer shell handle everything from a bluebird spring day to a cold January morning. Bring more thin layers and fewer bulky ones — the mountain temperature swings more than a single jacket can manage.

The unglamorous essentials decide your comfort: proper ski socks (thin and technical, not thick), good gloves plus a spare pair, a buff, and quality goggles with a low-light lens for grey days. They are small, easily forgotten, and a bad version of any of them costs you a day.

sections.title: The skin you will regret ignoring
paras: Altitude sun is fierce, reflected off the snow and stronger than it feels. High-factor sun cream, a proper lip balm with SPF, and aftersun are not optional — the most common holiday-photo regret is the goggle tan and the burnt lower face. Bring them; you will use them daily.

figure.alt: The ski-week essentials, laid out — Vertige
figure.cap: The ski-week essentials, laid out — Vertige

sections.title: What to arrange, not carry
paras: Skis and boots are the obvious one — at this level, equipment can be fitted and delivered to the chalet, so there is no reason to wrestle ski bags through airports unless you are attached to your own gear. The same goes for anything bulky or last-minute: it can usually be sorted in resort faster than packed.

This is where a good operator quietly earns its keep. Equipment fitting, lift passes, even forgotten kit — arranged before you arrive or fixed while you ski, so the packing list gets shorter and the holiday starts the moment you land.

sections.title: For the evenings
paras: Off the mountain, resorts are smart-casual rather than formal — bring something you would wear to a good dinner, and warm, sensible footwear with grip for icy streets. The mistake is over-packing for the evenings and under-packing for the snow. Reverse the instinct.

pick.text: Pack thin layers, not thick ones, and treat socks, gloves, goggles and sun cream as the things that actually decide your comfort — get those right and the rest is forgiving. Then arrange the bulky items in resort: have equipment fitted and waiting at the chalet rather than carried through three airports. The best-packed ski bag is a smaller one, because the operator handled everything that did not need to be in it.

title: The Vertige Journey
dek: Why we started, what we are building, and the one measure we care about.
sub: Why we started, what we are building, and the one measure we care about.
lead: Most ski companies will tell you they are different. Then they hand you the same list of chalets, in the same breathless language, as everyone else. We started Vertige because we thought the whole thing could be done with more honesty and more care — and because we had seen, from two very different angles, exactly where it falls down.

sections.title: Two angles on the same problem
paras: One of us, Oli, learned the mountain the only way you can — by living on it, as an instructor in Verbier, building the kind of knowledge that does not exist on a website. The other, Ben, builds brands and the systems behind them, and came to skiing late enough to remember what it is like to arrive not knowing anything.

Between those two views sits the whole business: the insider who knows which instructor is worth the money and which restaurant to book in October, and the builder who knows how to make a service actually deliver on what it promises. Plenty of companies have one. The combination is rarer.

sections.title: What we decided not to do
paras: We decided not to compete on volume. We do not want the longest list of chalets; we want the right ones, chosen carefully and presented honestly — told what they are and what they are not. We decided not to perform luxury, because the people we work with already know what they are paying for and can see through the act.

And we decided not to optimise every booking for margin. The money is better spent on the experience — the upgrade absorbed, the gift that arrives unasked, the call that goes further than it needed to. Some of the best things we do never appear on an invoice.

sections.title: The one number
paras: There is a single measure we care about above all others: the proportion of clients who come back the following year. Not bookings, not margin — returns. A holiday handled perfectly is the most powerful marketing there is, and everything about the business is designed to earn that next phone call.

sections.title: Where we are going
paras: We are building Vertige slowly and deliberately, the way the good things in this world get built — chalet by chalet, relationship by relationship, week by week. The ambition is not to be the biggest ski company, or even the most profitable. It is to be the one you never think to leave.

pick.text: If you want to understand Vertige in a sentence: we would rather have a hundred clients who come back every year than a thousand who book once. Everything else — the curation, the honesty, the service that goes further than it needed to — follows from that one choice. Come and ski with us once, and we intend to see you again next year.

title: Where to Eat in Verbier
dek: Where to eat in Verbier, from forest-hidden mountain huts to a Michelin-starred dining room. Our honest guide to the best restaurants, lunches and apres.
sub: Where to eat in Verbier, from forest-hidden mountain huts to a Michelin-starred dining room. Our honest guide to the best restaurants, lunches and apres.
lead: Knowing where to eat in Verbier is one of the more agreeable problems of a week in the resort. This is a town that takes its long lunches and its late nights equally seriously, offering everything from a forest-hidden mountain hut to a Michelin-starred dining room within a short ski, or a shorter walk. What follows is an honest shortlist, on the mountain and in the village, with a note on the tables worth booking well in advance.

sections.title: Where to eat in Verbier: the long mountain lunch
paras: The mountain lunch is a Verbier institution, and the best of them are worth planning your morning around. Chez Dany sits alone in the forest at Clambin, a ten-minute ski from Les Ruinettes or a twenty-minute walk, and rewards the detour with unfussy cooking a long way from the crowds. Nearer the pistes, La Marmotte serves the traditional dishes of the Val de Bagnes; the roestis, the fondues and the generous cheese boards, with a kitchen that stays open through the afternoon for anyone who arrives late. Le Carrefour is the address most regulars name first, celebrated for its hot-stone meats and a menu with a whole section given over to cheese; it runs two lunch sittings, at noon and at two, so booking is wise. For something sunnier and livelier, Bar 1936 and its yurts sit just below Les Ruinettes on the Mayentzet piste, with a terrace made for spring afternoons and the odd live set.

sections.title: The serious tables in the village
Apres, and the hours in between
paras: Few resorts blur lunch, apres and dinner as happily as Verbier. Le Rouge has done exactly that since 1971, a place where a long lunch on the terrace slides into an afternoon of resident DJs, with a guest list that has run from royalty to ski bums and an old-school glamour to match. The Pub Mont Fort remains the classic first stop off the slopes, its wide terrace looking down the valley and its crowd stylish but relaxed. And for the full, loud Verbier legend there is the Farinet, whose sliding roof opens over the main square for an apres-ski that involves rather more singing, and dancing on the bar, than sitting down; its adjoining Lounge answers the quieter mood with a serious gin and cocktail list.

pick.text: Book one long lunch at Chez Dany early in the week, skiing down to Clambin before the terraces fill; hold a table at La Table d'Adrien for the evening you want to remember; and leave one afternoon entirely unplanned for the Pub Mont Fort, because in Verbier the best nights tend to begin there by accident.
